reviewNotes: The complete evidence-reviewed graph has exactly fourteen relations: four ordered primary-path relations and ten supporting relations, each classified once.
The historical U-M 274-TIFF layer remains survival/access unknown; the only cached IA response is a 270-byte metadata-only dark-item record with target-work presence absent.
The Richmond carrier is the 1973 Kraus unaltered reprint, represented as catalog_description rather than a false new scholarly edition; its scan is not an original 1902 carrier.
Wright’s 1849 edition reaches Add MS 17376 through his now-unlocated handwritten working transcript; no flattened direct press-to-codex route is asserted.
Utrecht M113 coverage remains unknown even though the finding aid lists all seven incipits.
Kenyon’s publication is target-absent documentary context: its Add MS 17376 plate is f. 92, and its IA facsimile is separately modeled.

Hanna, Utrecht, Kenyon, DIMEV, METS, GND, law, and access-policy downloads are each visible through their own documentary entity/access layers with checksummed local copies; the locally generated deterministic audit is separately visible as a checksummed local-only entity.

The shared Add MS 17376 and Bodleian entity cores contain only intrinsic identity, holding, physical, date, and contents facts. BAU0088-specific loci, exclusions, and target-presence claims remain in relations, access, evidence, and questions.
Sibson-only precise custody, date, and 222-leaf claims remain unpromoted because the official thesis PDF was not frozen and directly inspected.
The U-M collection rights assertion, IA provider assertions, embedded Google contract notices, edition duration analyses, modern scan carriers, and repository-supplied image terms remain separate.

All forty frozen cache objects were independently rehashed and size-checked; thirty-nine downloaded objects appear as local_copies, and the one locally generated repo-source audit appears through local-only access and checksummed evidence.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is a pinned copy of University of Michigan item BAU0088.0001.001, a 2006 uncorrected OCR and Level 1 TEI encoding of a seven-poem selection from Matthias Konrath's 1902 EETS edition. The XML documents an immediate historical production layer of 274 consecutively named 600 dpi TIFF G4 page images; current survival and retrieval of that image set remain unverified, and an Internet Archive package endpoint is dark. Konrath re-edited the target poems directly from British Library Add MS 17376 with substantial editorial intervention and manuscript proof collation; his seven-poem text occupies pp.1-160, while the encoding omits the continuous notes on pp.161-246. Hanna identifies nine Latin leaves formerly at the front of Add MS 17376 part I as now separately preserved as Bodleian MS Lat. th. e. 32; that fragment contains no target poem. Thomas Wright's complete 1849 edition followed a now-unlocated working transcript he made while the poem portion was still privately held. A genuine original-1902 Konrath scan, a distinct 1973 Kraus unaltered-reprint scan, and a Wright 1849 scan are cached; the Richmond object is not an original 1902 carrier. A Utrecht finding aid lists microfilm M113 for Add MS 17376 and all seven incipits, but does not establish whole-codex completeness or image quality. Kenyon's public 1900 facsimile volume reproduces only f.92 in the Psalter, outside the target ff.150-220b. The current BL record has no Digitised Content link; no public whole-current-codex facsimile was verified as of 2026-07-17, and an exact Digital Bodleian search found no object for the detached fragment.

catalogingSubject.label: The Poems of William of Shoreham (seven-poem edited selection)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The edition-level content unit delimited by the pinned U-M XML: seven distinct Middle English religious poems from Matthias Konrath’s 1902 EETS edition, printed on pp. 1–160 and retained with in-text footnotes. It is not one medieval composition, Konrath’s complete printed volume, all works attributed to William of Shoreham, the whole of British Library Add MS 17376, the unavailable 274-TIFF production layer, or the later repository XML artifact.

contentParts.label: De septem sacramentis
notes: The sequence around f.162 reads 162, 164b, 163a, 163b, 164a, 164b.
The XML's final 182a milestone extends beyond DIMEV's f.181v endpoint.
contentParts.summary: A long didactic poem organized around the seven sacraments and their place in Christian teaching and pastoral instruction.

contentParts.label: On the Hours of the Cross
notes: The opening sequence reads 182b, 182a, 182b before proceeding to 183a.
The BL component label conflicts with the text-specific Konrath/DIMEV identification; both are preserved.
contentParts.summary: A devotional Hours poem structured around prayer and the Passion; Konrath and DIMEV identify the text as the Hours of the Cross, while the current BL catalog calls it Hours of the Virgin.

contentParts.label: De decem preceptis (The Ten Commandments)
notes: DIMEV cites Konrath pp.86-107; direct inspection of Konrath and the XML PB run gives pp.86-97.
contentParts.summary: A forty-three-stanza didactic treatment of the Ten Commandments and Christian ethical conduct.

contentParts.label: The Seven Deadly Sins
notes: DIMEV cites Konrath pp.98-14; direct inspection gives pp.98-114.
contentParts.summary: A 106-quatrain moral exposition of the seven deadly sins; one editorial alternate and one line nested in a note account for the XML’s two extra L descendants.

contentParts.label: The Five Joys of the Virgin Mary
notes: DIMEV cites Konrath pp.15-26; direct inspection gives pp.115-126.
contentParts.summary: A Marian devotional poem on the Five Joys, including a brief embedded Latin biblical or liturgical quotation near its close.

contentParts.label: Marye mayde mylde and fre
notes: DIMEV cites Wright pp.113-14; direct inspection gives pp.131-134.
Traditional Shoreham attribution remains disputed; no Grosseteste source composition or historical exemplar is identified.
contentParts.summary: A fourteen-stanza Marian hymn traditionally associated with William of Shoreham; its closing notice invokes Robert Grosseteste, leaving authorship and source responsibility unresolved.

contentParts.label: Exposition of Christian Doctrine
notes: DIMEV record 2517 has the correct incipit heading, title, locus, line count, and editions, but its displayed witness first/last lines belong to an unrelated poem and must not be propagated.
The sequence around f.212 reads 211b, 212b, 212b, 213a; no 212a milestone appears.

The only known codex breaks off before the promised treatment of redemption; no final homily, invocation, or colophon survives. This records witness/textual incompleteness and does not claim whether William ever completed the work elsewhere.
contentParts.summary: An incomplete exposition treating the Trinity, creation, evil, devils, Adam and Eve, the Fall, and original sin; the surviving text ends before its announced continuation.

dateStatements.display: after 1320 and before 1333 (cautious Shoreham-associated activity window)
notes: Konrath derives only an activity association from William’s Chart Sutton vicarage and Archbishop Mepham’s death; this is not one composition date and excludes disputed part VI.

extent.description: Seven count-once verse poems. The XML has 4,520 L descendants, but part IV contains one direct editorial alternate plus one additional L nested in a note; the qualified conventional count is therefore 4,518 line positions. The 709 LG containers are representation groups, and part II is structurally varied rather than uniformly stanzaic.

formStatements.description: All seven selected poems are verse; XML LG containers are edition structures and are not uniformly equivalent to stanzas.

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: The catalog-summary language list is the discovery union of modeled statements, not a claim that Latin is a whole-work co-primary language or that the selection is uniformly bilingual. Middle English remains whole-scope and primary; Latin is embedded paratext and source-phrase material scoped across the seven parts. Direct non-note XML text includes “Et benedictus fructus ventris Tui, iesus” in part V and “Quicunque uult” in part VII.

scope.description: Brief headings, colophons, and source phrases across all seven selected poems

notes: Latin headings, colophons, and brief source phrases are scoped to all seven parts. Middle English alone is whole-scope and primary; including Latin in the catalog-summary discovery union does not make the selection uniformly bilingual.
The same physical codex is relevant to another corpus item, but this record makes no claims for that item and binds only BAU0088’s seven poem parts through scoped lineage relations.
The detached Bodleian fragment contains no selected poem and remains supporting carrier context rather than a witness to this selection.
The Princeton Scheide Psalter tradition is excluded because it concerns the codex’s Psalter material, not these seven poems.

DIMEV’s documented page errors, unrelated part-VII displayed first/last lines, and the XML’s non-monotonic folio milestones are preserved as source-layer problems rather than normalized manuscript truth.
The “Part I” publication label does not establish that a completed Konrath Part II exists.

summaries.value: A seven-poem edited selection of Middle English religious verse associated with William of Shoreham, vicar of Chart Sutton in Kent. It joins catechetical and pastoral teaching on the sacraments, Ten Commandments, deadly sins, and Christian doctrine with an Hours poem and two Marian works. The sixth poem’s authorship or source remains disputed because its closing notice invokes Robert Grosseteste; the seventh survives incompletely. The repository XML is a 2006 uncorrected OCR of Konrath’s 1902 edition, not a direct manuscript transcription.
